critic_raw:
The stratified random split on radiology reports from a hospital EHR system ignores the patient-level clustering structure. Multiple reports likely exist per patient, and a random split will place different reports from the same patient in both train and test sets. This is a direct form of data leakage: the model trains on radiologist language patterns, disease co-occurrence profiles, and stylistic tendencies specific to individual patients and radiologists, and then evaluates on holdout reports from those same entities. Stratifying by disease code does not address this; it only ensures label balance, not independence.

Second, the rule-based baseline uses 'keyword extraction,' which is a weak and arguably strawman comparator for multi-label ICD coding. The state-of-practice baseline in clinical NLP coding is not simple keyword lookup but rather rule-based systems like MetaMap, cTAKES, or institutional coding engines that incorporate negation detection, context handling, and medical ontologies. Using a naive keyword baseline systematically overstates the transformer advantage by selecting the weakest available comparator.

Third, Macro-F1 is listed as the primary metric for a multi-label classification task with disease codes. Disease code distributions in radiology are highly skewed — common findings (e.g., normal studies, consolidation) dominate, rare codes appear in <1% of reports. Macro-F1 weights all classes equally, but for rare ICD codes, the denominator of the F1 computation will be tiny (few true positives in the test set), making the metric high-variance and sensitive to one or two predictions on rare classes. The design does not discuss a minimum support threshold or how to handle zero-support classes in macro averaging.

Fourth, the design controls for 'departmental coding practices' but does not address radiologist-level variation. Individual radiologists have systematic stylistic differences that a fine-tuned transformer will learn to exploit (associating language style with code patterns). If test reports come from the same radiologists as training reports — which a random split guarantees — this constitutes a radiologist-identity confound that inflates performance beyond what would generalize to new radiologists or institutions.

Fifth, the design fine-tunes on 10,000 reports, which is a relatively small corpus for transformer fine-tuning on multi-label tasks with a potentially large label space (ICD-10 has tens of thousands of codes). The design does not specify the label space size, making it impossible to assess whether the evaluation setup is feasible or whether the model is being evaluated on a filtered subset of common codes only.
all_issues_raised: No patient-level or radiologist-level split: multiple reports per patient mean a random split places different reports from the same patient in train and test, directly leaking patient-specific language patterns and disease profiles into evaluation.

The keyword-extraction rule-based baseline is a strawman; clinical NLP systems in practice use negation-aware, ontology-grounded engines (MetaMap, cTAKES), and the comparison against naive keyword matching systematically overstates transformer gains.

Macro-F1 on a skewed multi-label ICD coding task is high-variance because rare disease codes have tiny test-set support; without a minimum-support threshold or handling of zero-support classes, the metric is unreliable for rare labels.

Radiologist-identity confound: transformers learn idiosyncratic writing styles associated with code patterns; a random split ensures test reports come from the same radiologists as training, inflating estimated generalization performance.

The label space size (ICD-10 has tens of thousands of codes) is unspecified, making it unclear whether the experiment evaluates all codes or a filtered common subset, which changes the difficulty and meaning of the Macro-F1 result fundamentally.
all_issues_adjudicated: No patient-level or radiologist-level split: multiple reports per patient mean a random split places different reports from the same patient in train and test, directly leaking patient-specific language patterns and disease profiles into evaluation.

The keyword-extraction rule-based baseline is a strawman; clinical NLP systems in practice use negation-aware, ontology-grounded engines (MetaMap, cTAKES), and the comparison against naive keyword matching systematically overstates transformer gains.

Macro-F1 on a skewed multi-label ICD coding task is high-variance because rare disease codes have tiny test-set support; without a minimum-support threshold or handling of zero-support classes, the metric is unreliable for rare labels.

Radiologist-identity confound: transformers learn idiosyncratic writing styles associated with code patterns; a random split ensures test reports come from the same radiologists as training, inflating estimated generalization performance.

The label space size (ICD-10 has tens of thousands of codes) is unspecified, making it unclear whether the experiment evaluates all codes or a filtered common subset, which changes the difficulty and meaning of the Macro-F1 result fundamentally.
